beats.text: Reinforcement learning studies an agent that learns by interacting with an environment it cannot directly control.
At each step the agent observes a state, chooses an action, and the environment returns a reward and a new state.
Unlike a label that names the right answer, a reward is only evaluative: it scores the action but never reveals the best one.

visual.label: reward is a single scalar per step

beats.text: Feedback is also delayed, so the agent must solve credit assignment, deciding which past decision earned a late reward.
The data is not fixed: the agent's own policy generates the states it sees, so improving the policy changes the data.
The reward hypothesis claims any goal can be cast as maximizing expected cumulative scalar reward.
The Markov decision process formalizes the interaction loop and is the common language for planning and learning.
An MDP is a tuple of states, actions, a transition function, a reward function, and a discount factor.

visual.note: five objects fully specify the world

beats.text: The Markov property says the current state is a sufficient statistic, so the future is independent of the past.

visual.tex: P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, \ldots, s_0) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)
visual.note: history collapses into the current state

beats.text: Markovness is a property of how you define the state: stack four video frames and a single frame becomes Markov.
Transitions and rewards together form the model, and whether the agent knows them splits planning from learning.
In a gridworld the discount is 0.9, moving up succeeds 90 percent of the time, and each step costs 0.04.

visual.label: P(intended move) in the slippery grid

beats.text: The agent maximizes the return, the total reward from now to the end, not just the immediate reward.
The discounted return weights a reward k steps ahead by gamma to the k, keeping infinite sums finite.
Three consecutive rewards of one at gamma 0.9 give a return of 1 plus 0.9 plus 0.81, which is 2.71.
Factoring out one gamma gives the recursion: this step's reward plus the discounted return of everything after.

visual.note: the seed of every Bellman equation

beats.text: The weight gamma to the k decays into a long tail as gamma rises, stretching how far the agent looks ahead.
The effective horizon one over one minus gamma is about 10 steps at 0.9 and about 100 at 0.99.
A policy maps states to actions and is the object reinforcement learning searches for, the solution to the MDP.

visual.sub: behavior, and how good it is

beats.text: A stochastic policy is a distribution over actions given the state, with the probabilities summing to one.
The value functions are expected returns: V from a state under the policy, Q from a state after a chosen action.

visual.tex: V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s], \quad Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]

beats.text: The state-value is the policy-weighted average of action-values, so weight on the better action raises V.
Knowing Q lets you act greedily without a model, which is why model-free methods learn Q rather than V.
Values are always relative to a policy, except the optimal value function, the best achievable from each state.

visual.note: solve the MDP, read off the policy

beats.text: Exploration versus exploitation is the dilemma with no counterpart in supervised learning.
Exploit and you take the action you believe is best; explore and you try uncertain actions that might be better.
Because the agent's actions decide its data, an action never taken is an action never learned from.
Epsilon-greedy takes the greedy action most of the time and a uniformly random action with probability epsilon.
With epsilon 0.1 over four actions the greedy action gets 0.925, leaving 0.075 split among the rest.
Epsilon is usually annealed from high to low, and smarter schemes like UCB direct exploration toward real uncertainty.
